mnemonics: DECODERS: Don't Expect Common Outputs; Each Selector Reveals One
BINARY INPUTS: Because Inputs Naturally Yield Outputs (Selectively)
ACTIVE LOW: Always Check That Inputs Lead Outputs (When Low)
keyPoints: A decoder activates exactly one output line for each unique binary input combination.
An n-to-2^n decoder has n inputs and 2^n outputs, with only one output active (high or low) at any time.
The 'enable' input acts as a master switch; the decoder only functions when the enable condition is met.
commonMistakes: Assuming multiple outputs can be active at the same time for a single input code.
Confusing the function of a decoder (input selects output) with a multiplexer (select input chooses which data input passes to output).
Forgetting that outputs can be active-low (often indicated by a bubble on the output pin), meaning a '0' represents the active state.
